content: Model grounded in Rangel (2013) hormonal modulation and Jacquier (2016) ghrelin/leptin dynamics.
Attribute-based value computation proposes that the subjective value of a choice option is a weighted linear sum of its attribute-level values: V(o) = Σ wᵢ·aᵢ(o).
The vmPFC/OFC encodes the overall weighted-sum value signal; higher vmPFC BOLD activity correlates parametrically with subjective value and decreases after outcome devaluation.
Attribute-based computation is the algorithm of the goal-directed valuation system, enabling valuation of novel outcomes by projecting them onto familiar attribute dimensions.
Immediate/basic attributes (taste, hedonic impact) and abstract/delayed attributes (health, long-term cost) are qualitatively distinct classes that receive systematically different weights.
The attentional allocation variable αₐₜₜ(i) modulates attribute weight wᵢ multiplicatively; increasing attention to an attribute raises its contribution to the overall value V(o).

Disruption of dlPFC (via TMS, lesion, or cognitive load) selectively reduces the contribution of abstract/delayed attributes to vmPFC value signals, biasing choices toward immediately rewarding options.
Attribute conflict (high taste, low health) produces longer reaction times and greater dlPFC–vmPFC coactivation compared to attribute-aligned trials.
The goal-directed system's value is sensitive to outcome devaluation: V_GD updates immediately when either action–outcome contingency or outcome desirability changes.
The habitual system computes V_H via incremental TD-learning without encoding an explicit action–outcome model; V_H does not update following outcome devaluation without re-experience.
The DDM models the decision process as dX(t) = v·dt + σ·dW(t); a response is executed when X(t) first reaches an absorbing boundary.
Drift rate v scales with stimulus coherence; higher coherence produces faster and more accurate responses in the DDM.
The DDM with fixed boundaries and zero drift variability is mathematically equivalent to the optimal sequential probability ratio test (SPRT).
White-matter connectivity between pre-SMA and striatum predicts individual differences in DDM boundary parameter a, linking anatomy to speed-accuracy control.

Ghrelin, produced by the stomach, is a short-term orexigenic hormone that increases food intake; its secretion rises during fasting and is suppressed by food consumption. Beyond driving hunger, ghrelin also amplifies food incentive salience and modulates vmPFC food value encoding.

Leptin is a long-term anorexigenic hormone secreted proportionally to fat mass; it suppresses food intake by inhibiting hypothalamic AgRP/NPY neurons in the arcuate nucleus and through mesolimbic pathways (VTA, NAcc).
Pavlovian food-approach responses are triggered by food-predictive cues without deliberative action–outcome computation; they are driven by cached stimulus values and scale with hunger state.
Mesolimbic dopamine from VTA to NAcc encodes reward prediction errors (δ = r − V) that update Pavlovian CS values; phasic bursts at CS onset drive learning of CS–food associations.
Incentive salience ('wanting') is a dopamine-mediated motivational signal driving approach to food cues, dissociable from opioidergic hedonic 'liking' generated during consumption.

The dorsomedial striatum represents action-outcome associations underlying goal-directed (model-based) control in the RL framework; the dorsolateral striatum encodes stimulus-response associations underlying habitual (model-free) control.
Basal ganglia subthalamic nucleus stimulation in DBS patients reduces DDM boundary parameter a, increasing response impulsivity and confirming a role in boundary/threshold setting.

Model implements three-system architecture: goal-directed controller (model-based, hunger-sensitive), habitual controller (model-free Q-learning, hunger-insensitive), and Pavlovian override (cue-driven eat response when food at cell).
Arbitration weight omega shifts from goal-directed toward habitual as eating experience accumulates.
Pavlovian system injects fixed probability p_pav of eating whenever food is present at the agent's cell.

Formulation.drift-diffusion-model:algebraic-closed-form-ddm-with-softmax-action-selection -[HAS_PARAMETER]-> Parameter.drift-diffusion-model:algebraic-closed-form-ddm-with-softmax-action-selection:sigma
Goal-directed system computes hunger-sensitive action values using r_food / (1 + distance_to_nearest_food) for move actions.
Habitual system uses temporal difference Q-learning with unmodulated reward (r_food or c_step independent of hunger state).
Arbitration weight computed as omega = max(omega_0 - lambda_omega * n_eat, 0.0) to implement habitization.
Integrated action values computed as V_int[a] = omega * V_GD[a] + (1 - omega) * Q_H[s, a].
Final action probabilities use softmax over integrated values with Pavlovian override boost to eat action when food at cell.
State representation is discrete: (x, y, food_at_cell, dx_sign, dy_sign) where dx_sign and dy_sign point toward nearest food.
Energy dynamics update: E = clamp(E - alpha_E + c_food * ate, 0, 1).
Hunger dynamics update: H = clamp(H + eta_H - kappa_H * ate, 0, 1).
Model detects successful eating via last_action_result keys: consumed, success, food_consumed, result='consumed', or outcome='consumed'.
Uses Rescorla-Wagner TD(0) prediction-error rule for pavlovian state value learning
Implements softmax action selection over hunger-modulated Pavlovian action values
Maintains cached state values V(s) for each grid cell position
Computes reward prediction error as delta_t = reward - V(current_position)
Applies hunger dynamics with increment rate alpha_h and satiation decrement gamma_sat
Movement cost c_step is subtracted from Pavlovian action values for non-stay actions
Eat action produces value modulated by hunger level and state value: mu * h_t * V(pos)
Hunger level is bounded in range [0.0, 1.0]
State values are clipped to range [0.0, R_max]
Action probabilities are computed via softmax and sampled using weighted random selection
Implements three-method DecisionModel contract: decide() (read-only), update() (state mutation), and get_state()
Model maintains separate wanting (dopamine-mediated) and liking (opioid-mediated) value maps with independent learning dynamics.
Wanting uses fast learning rate (alpha_W = 0.12); liking uses slow learning rate (alpha_L = 0.05).
Hunger multiplicatively gates wanting contribution (Q_W = mu * h_t * W) and modulates liking via alliesthesia (Q_L = h_lam * L).
Combined action value uses weighted sum: Q_total = w_W * Q_W + w_L * Q_L minus movement cost (c_step).
Formulation.drift-diffusion-model:algebraic-closed-form-ddm-with-softmax-action-selection -[USES_VARIABLE]-> Variable.drift-diffusion-model:bart-i

Formulation.drift-diffusion-model:collapsing-boundary-ode-accumulators-with-lateral-inhibition -[HAS_PARAMETER]-> Parameter.drift-diffusion-model:collapsing-boundary-ode-accumulators-with-lateral-inhibition:a
Action selection uses softmax with inverse temperature beta = 5.0.
Wanting prediction error: delta_W = reward - W(pos).
Liking prediction error incorporates alliesthesia: delta_L = reward * h_lam - L(pos).
Hunger dynamics follow: h_t = h_t + alpha_h - gamma_sat * ate, bounded to [0, 1].
Movement actions masked if outside grid bounds; eat action masked if no food at current position.
Wanting and liking values clipped to [0, W_max] and [0, L_max] respectively.
decide() method is read-only, samples action using current hunger level without modifying state.
update() method applies all learning rules (R1–R5) and updates cached Q_total and action probabilities.
Model ode-based-continuous-drive-dynamics-with-deterministic-threshold-policy implements continuous-time Pavlovian agent modeled via ODEs governing hunger drive and associative strength.
Model uses logistic growth of hunger drive toward 1 with sharp drop on eating via rule R1: dH/dt = c_H(1-H) - k_sat*ate.
Model implements Rescorla-Wagner learning with explicit extinction decay for associative strength V(s).
Model uses deterministic threshold policy on effective motivational drive E(t) = mu * H_t * V_perceived.
Model computes V_perceived as maximum associative strength among visible cells (current position, food cells, and valid neighbors).
Model implements gradient ascent on V landscape: moves toward neighbor with highest V value when E exceeds approach threshold.
Model implements four-level decision hierarchy: hunger override (H > 0.8 eats unconditionally), eat if E >= theta_eat, approach if E >= theta_approach, otherwise stay.
Model passes 13 behavior tests verifying hunger dynamics, Rescorla-Wagner learning, gradient ascent, and threshold hierarchy.
Model uses Euler discretization with dt=1.0 for ODE integration of hunger dynamics.
Model maintains sparse dictionary of associative strengths keyed by (x,y) coordinates.
Model computes perceived value as maximum V across all visible cells including current cell and valid movement neighbors.
Model uses clamping to ensure H_t stays in [0,1] range after each update.
Model implements extinction as multiplicative decay: V -= eta*V for unreinforced cells.
Model uses random tie-breaking in gradient ascent when multiple neighbors have equal V values.

The pipeline has instantiated a comprehensive multi-paradigm computational library of food-decision models (6+ paradigms: attribute-based value computation, dlPFC self-control, goal-directed vs. habitual control, Pavlovian food approach, homeostatic regulation, drift-diffusion) each with multiple competing formulations (algebraic, ODE, Bayesian, DDM variants), all grounded in Rangel (2013) and Rangel, Camerer & Montague (2008) postulates — future runs should prioritize cross-paradigm integration tests (e.g., linking DDM decision time to dlPFC depletion, or uncertainty-based arbitration to attribute conflict signals) rather than expanding individual formulation coverage.

A consistent but unresolved parameter conflict exists across formulations: key values such as α_E (basal metabolic cost: 0.01 vs. 0.08), c_food (energy gain: 0.30 vs. 3.0), c_step (step cost: −0.02 vs. −0.05 vs. 0.01), σ (diffusion coefficient: 0.1 vs. 1.0), γ (discount: 0.90 vs. 0.95), and α (learning rate: 0.1 vs. 0.15) are reported with contradictory defaults across runs — future pipeline runs must enforce a canonical parameter registry with provenance tagging to prevent these cross-formulation inconsistencies from propagating into downstream behavioral benchmarks.

A stochastic lapse rate of ε = 0.1 is consistently used across pipeline observations to model two mechanistically distinct but operationally unified sources of behavioral variability — motor execution noise and random exploration — via ε-greedy action selection, suggesting this parameter conflates rather than separates these contributions.

Future research runs should consider decomposing the single ε parameter into separate components for motor noise and exploration (e.g., a mixture model or hierarchical parameterization) to isolate their independent effects on decision-making behavior.

All three facts describe the same linear normalization transformation mapping food palatability from its original range [0.1, 1.0] to the standardized interval [-1, 1] via the formula a_abs = 2 * (palatability - 0.1) / 0.9 - 1; this is a single, fully redundant finding with no new information added across the three entries. Future pipeline runs should implement deduplication logic to collapse algebraically identical formulas into a single canonical fact before clustering.

The model architecture consistently employs continuous-time ODE systems discretized via Euler integration for both state evolution and homeostatic regulation, suggesting a unified numerical framework where biological/dynamical processes are formulated in continuous time but approximated at discrete grid steps — a design choice that prioritizes mathematical naturalism over discrete-native formulations but introduces Euler truncation error as a potential confound.

The recurrence of Euler discretization across multiple subsystems (state evolution and homeostatic ODEs) implies that the accuracy and stability of the overall model is globally sensitive to step-size selection; future research runs should systematically evaluate higher-order integration schemes (e.g., Runge-Kutta) or adaptive step-size methods to assess whether Euler approximation error accumulates differentially across subsystems.

The conflict signal K is formally defined as the scalar value discrepancy between the tastiest and healthiest food options (K = V_taste_max − V_health_max), functioning as a zero-valued signal when both options coincide and a positive signal proportional to the taste advantage when they diverge — making it a precise, continuous measure of goal conflict rather than a binary indicator.

This operationalization of K provides a computationally actionable conflict metric that can be used to parameterize decision arbitration models, where the magnitude of K could modulate the weighting between taste-driven and health-driven choice systems across experimental conditions.

The perception interface is highly consistent across pipeline runs: agent position (x, y), food resources with palatability, food-at-cell boolean (computed by coordinate matching), and grid dimensions are reliably extracted from a standardized perception dict/object, making this a stable input contract for any decision model built on top of it.

content: Equation 1 computes effective attribute weights by normalizing scaled allocations of immediate and abstract attributes, where hunger increases weight on immediate attributes via multiplicative factor (1 + γ_H · H_t).
Equation 2 computes overall option value as a weighted linear combination of immediate and abstract attribute values, with weights determined by hunger state and attention.

Equation 3 maps actions to option values: an action's value equals the value of the target option if reachable, or zero otherwise; immediate attribute is proximity-scaled desirability and abstract attribute is nutritional quality.
Equation 5 updates hunger state: hunger increases by rate η per timestep and is reduced by R_food when eating occurs, bounded in [0, 1].

Equation 6 updates attention allocations using a reward prediction error signal: if expected value V_t underestimates received reward r_t, attention to attribute i increases by λ · (r_t - V_t) · a_i(o_t), then normalizes with floor ε.

The inverse temperature β (softmax inverse temperature) defaults to 5.0, representing moderate exploration, and is sourced from prior pipeline calibrations with a typical range of 1–10. It controls action selection stochasticity: higher β increases exploitation of high-value actions, producing more deterministic action selection; lower β produces more stochastic exploration.
Parameter γ_H (hunger influence) defaults to 0.6 and amplifies the weight of immediate attributes when hunger is high; derived from Rangel (2013).
Parameter δ (temporal discount) defaults to 0.7 and down-weights abstract attributes relative to immediate ones, reflecting bounded control; sourced from Rangel (2013).
Parameter η (hunger decay rate) defaults to 0.01 and represents a slow metabolic timescale in simulation convention.
Parameter R_food (hunger restoration) defaults to 0.3 and quantifies the hunger reduction from eating in simulation convention.
Parameter N (number of attributes) defaults to 2 and represents the canonical pair of taste and health; sourced from Rangel (2013).

Selects actions via softmax distribution over computed utilities parameterized by inverse temperature β (Equation 4), implementing stochastic choice proportional to expected utility, consistent with expected-utility-theory.
Equation 1 (Attribute weights) computes hunger-modulated attribute importance, ensuring immediate-attribute weight increases monotonically with hunger state H_t and weights sum to 1.
Equation 2 (Drift rate) computes the mean evidence accumulation rate for each action as a weighted sum of its immediate and abstract attribute values.
Equation 3 (Attribute conflict) measures the absolute difference between an option's subjective value on immediate versus abstract attributes, quantifying decision conflict.
Equation 4 (Noise magnitude) scales stochastic noise in evidence accumulation multiplicatively by attribute conflict, linking decision uncertainty to value disagreement.
Equation 5 (Evidence accumulation) is a discrete-time drift-diffusion process where each action's evidence accumulator increases by drift rate plus independent Gaussian noise.
Equation 6 (Action selection) implements threshold-based decision rule: the first action whose accumulator reaches decision threshold θ is chosen; if none reaches θ by T_max steps, argmax is used.
Equation 7 (Attribute weight learning) updates attribute importance post-decision using reward prediction error (RPE) and the selected action's attribute values, with learning rate β_w.
Equation 8 (Hunger dynamics) updates hunger state via constant decay rate η and reduction by hunger restoration R_food when eating occurs.
Parameter θ (Decision threshold) is set to 1.0 following standard drift-diffusion model calibration; agent commits to action when evidence accumulator E_a reaches this value.
Parameter σ_0 (Base noise) is set to 0.3, representing moderate stochasticity in evidence accumulation consistent with typical DDM calibrations.
Parameter κ (Conflict-noise scaling) is set to 0.5, derived from Rangel (2013) empirical finding that attribute conflict increases both reaction time and decision noise.
Parameter T_max (Maximum deliberation steps) is set to 10 as a simulation constraint modeling bounded rationality and finite deliberation capacity.
Parameter β_w (Weight learning rate) is set to 0.05, consistent with standard reinforcement learning rates for policy/weight updates.
Parameter η (Hunger decay rate) is set to 0.01 and is inherited from Formulation 1 for consistency in homeostatic dynamics.
Parameter R_food (Hunger restoration) is set to 0.3 and is inherited from Formulation 1 to maintain consistent hunger satisfaction mechanics.
The formulation integrates drift-diffusion decision dynamics with attribute-based value computation and hunger-driven motivation modulation.
Hungry agents (high H_t) show stronger preference for immediate-attribute values; satiated agents (low H_t) weight abstract attributes more heavily.
High attribute conflict increases decision uncertainty (noise) and reaction time, predicting slower choices when immediate and abstract attributes disagree.
Hunger dynamics (Eq. 1): hunger H rises at rate η_H and is reduced by R_food when eating occurs, modeling metabolic drive with homeostatic restoration.
Cognitive control dynamics (Eq. 2): control K passively recovers toward resting level K_0 with time constant τ_K and is depleted by c_K per decision step, implementing ego-depletion.
Target immediate attribute weight (Eq. 3): w_imm^* increases with hunger H and decreases with cognitive control K, capturing the competition between physiological drive and self-regulation.
Target abstract attribute weight (Eq. 3): w_abs^* increases with cognitive control K (amplified by φ) and decreases with hunger H, enabling dlPFC to up-weight health-related attributes.
Weight dynamics (Eq. 4): attribute weights w_i track their target values w_i^* with exponential relaxation time constant τ_w, introducing inertia to weight changes.
Option value computation (Eq. 5): V(o) is a weighted linear sum of immediate and abstract attributes, combining multiple value dimensions.

Q-value temporal-difference update (Eq. 6): Q-values update toward the bootstrapped target r_t + γ·max_a' Q(a'), learning action values through experience with learning rate α_Q and discount factor γ.
Blended action value (Eq. 7): final decision value U(a) averages goal-directed attribute value V(o_a) with learned Q-value, combining dual-system processes.
Action selection (Eq. 8): actions are sampled from a softmax distribution over U(a), with inverse temperature β controlling exploration-exploitation trade-off.

Run bd5e6d0d-382f-46f9-9900-2994e0d5eed3 contradicted memory c6050eb4-220b-4e88-b67d-7f95c8b04f7d: Action selection uses a softmax policy over integrated values (Eq. 7): P(a|s) = exp(β·V_GD^(d)(a,s)) / Σ_j exp(β·V_GD^(d)(j,s)), where inverse temperature β controls the randomness of choice and the exploration-exploitation tradeoff. → Action selection (Eq. 8): actions are sampled from a softmax distribution over U(a), with inverse temperature β controlling exploration-exploitation trade-off.
Parameter η_H (hunger rise rate, default 0.01) sets the timescale of metabolic need accumulation per step.
Parameter R_food (hunger restoration, default 0.3) determines how much eating reduces hunger, calibrated to simulation timescale.
Parameter τ_K (control recovery time constant, default 50.0) reflects the slow and effortful recovery of dlPFC engagement from ego-depletion.
Parameter K_0 (resting control, default 0.5) sets the baseline self-regulation capacity when not depleted.
Parameter c_K (control depletion per step, default 0.02) models the cognitive cost of abstract attribute processing.
Parameter φ (control boost, default 1.5) reflects how much dlPFC engagement amplifies abstract attribute weighting, per Rangel (2013).
Parameter τ_w (weight dynamics time constant, default 5.0) determines the speed at which attribute weights adapt to state changes.

Parameter β (softmax inverse temperature, default 5.0) from comparison process P6 is the standard action selection parameter controlling the steepness of softmax action selection and governing the stochasticity of choice: higher β increases exploitation (less stochastic), while lower β increases exploration (more stochastic).
Parameter α_Q (Q-learning rate, default 0.1) is the standard reinforcement learning parameter controlling credit assignment speed.
Parameter γ (discount factor, default 0.9) is the standard temporal discounting parameter weighting future rewards.
Habit values V_I(a,s) are updated via temporal-difference learning with learning rate α_Q = 0.1 and reward prediction error (r_received − V_I(a,s)).

Formulation basis: The ODE-dynamic formulation integrates continuous-time weight dynamics with homeostatic hunger regulation and dlPFC-driven cognitive control, implementing Rangel (2013) on top-down modulation and Rangel, Camerer & Montague (2008) on goal-directed valuation.
Conflict signal K(t) computes the value discrepancy between the tastiest action and the healthiest action, equaling zero when both agree.
dlPFC coupling strength C(t) uses sigmoid of conflict signal scaled by λ_K, modulated multiplicatively by goal activation G(t) and inverse depletion (1 − D(t)).
Health weight w_h(t) increases linearly with dlPFC coupling via gain parameter γ_C; taste weight w_τ(t) is constrained to sum with health weight to 1.
Composite Chosen Value CCV(a) is a linear weighted combination of an action's taste and health attributes using current weights w_τ(t) and w_h(t).
Depletion D(t) increases when dlPFC coupling is active (C(t) > 0) at rate α_D and passively recovers at rate β_D when coupling is inactive.
Goal activation G(t) decays exponentially at rate η_G each step and receives a boost of 0.3 when the agent successfully chooses the healthy option.
Baseline health weight w_h0 = 0.2 reflects vmPFC encoding bias toward taste attributes without dlPFC engagement.
Baseline taste weight w_τ0 = 0.8 reflects hedonic dominance in vmPFC valuation absent top-down control.
Coupling gain γ_C = 0.6 amplifies the effect of dlPFC engagement on health attribute weight per Rangel postulate P1.
Depletion rate α_D = 0.08 per control event approximates the energy cost of sustained dlPFC engagement.
Passive recovery rate β_D = 0.04 is approximately half the depletion rate, reflecting slower passive restoration of dlPFC resources.
Conflict sensitivity λ_K = 1.5 determines how sharply dlPFC engages in response to increased goal-taste conflict per postulate P4.
Goal decay rate η_G = 0.05 models exponential decay of goal activation without reinforcement.
boost(t) = 0.3 reinforces goal representation when the agent selects the healthy option, strengthening long-term goal encoding.
Executive capacity E(t) evolves as a first-order ODE with recovery dynamics proportional to (1 − E) and depletion proportional to control exertion ctrl(t).
Impulsive action value V_I(a,s) is computed as a Pavlovian/habitual value weighted by taste of the resource and temporal discount γ^d(a) over Manhattan distance d(a).
Goal-directed action value V_G(a,s) is a linear combination of taste (weight w_τ^G = 0.4) and health (weight w_h^G = 0.6) outcomes, discounted by temporal distance.
The arbitration weight ω(t) is the square of executive capacity E(t)^2, ensuring nonlinear collapse of goal-directed influence under severe depletion.
Integrated value V(a,s) is a convex mixture of goal-directed and impulsive values, with weight ω(t) on goal-directed and (1 − ω(t)) on impulsive.
Control exertion ctrl(t) is a binary conflict indicator: 1 if the argmax impulsive action differs from argmax goal-directed action, else 0.

The decision logic operates in a sequence: perceive state, update habits, compute both value systems, detect conflict, update capacity, recompute arbitration weight, compute integrated values, then execute ε-greedy action selection.
Equation 1 computes the impulsive policy via softmax over taste-weighted, discounted action values, reflecting Pavlovian/habitual preference for hedonic stimuli.
Equation 2 computes the goal-directed policy via softmax over a weighted combination of taste and health attributes, reflecting deliberative value integration.
Equation 3 detects conflict as a binary event: likelihood is high (0.9) when the greedy actions under impulsive and goal-directed policies diverge, and low (0.1) otherwise.
Equation 4 implements Bayesian belief updating of conflict probability conditioned on the conflict likelihood signal, with subsequent smoothing via learning rate φ.
Equation 5 gates dlPFC engagement probability as the product of conflict belief and available executive capacity, capturing resource-dependent modulation.
Equation 6 forms the final mixture policy by interpolating between goal-directed and impulsive policies weighted by engagement probability.
Equation 7 governs executive capacity dynamics: recovery via β_E, depletion proportional to engagement effort (α_E), bounded in [0, 1].
Equation 8 computes effective reward by combining immediate taste and health outcomes minus metabolic control cost scaled by engagement probability.
Parameter b_0 = 0.3 sets the base rate of conflict belief, calibrated from empirical data showing ~30% of food choices involve goal–impulse disagreement.
Parameter β_I = 8.0 controls impulsive softmax temperature, higher than β_G to promote exploitation of immediate hedonic values.
Parameter β_G = 4.0 controls goal-directed softmax temperature, lower than β_I to encourage prudent exploration of health-weighted alternatives.
Parameter α_E = 0.06 is the executive depletion rate, controlling how rapidly engagement drains capacity toward exhaustion.
Parameter κ = 0.02 quantifies the metabolic cost of dlPFC engagement per unit, reflecting the energy demands of goal-directed control.
Parameter φ = 0.3 sets the Bayesian learning rate for belief updating, implementing moderate responsiveness to new conflict evidence.
Parameter w_h = 0.5 weights health attributes in the goal-directed policy, implementing Rangel's (2013) multi-attribute preference model.
Parameter w_τ = 1.0 weights taste in the impulsive policy exclusively, implementing the Pavlovian subsystem's hedonic focus.
The formulation implements graceful degradation: as executive capacity depletes, the mixture progressively favors the impulsive policy without hard threshold failure.
Conflict belief dynamically adapts to environment statistics: in stable environments with low actual conflict, posterior belief b(t) converges toward low values.
The framework integrates active inference by minimizing expected free energy through conflict-aware policy selection gated by available resources.

Equation 1 (drift rate computation) combines proximity-based evidence from resources, a baseline component for the eat action when a resource is present, and exponentially-weighted reward history into a drift rate v_i that drives evidence accumulation toward that action.

Equation 2 (evidence accumulation) models the discrete Wiener process where each accumulator X_i evolves by adding a deterministic drift term (v_i * Δt), scaled noise (σ * sqrt(Δt) * ξ_i(t)), and is clamped to [0, a] with reflecting boundary at 0 and absorbing boundary at a.

Equation 3 (boundary-crossing decision rule) selects the chosen action R as the one whose accumulator first reaches the decision threshold a, implementing a race model with a first-to-threshold stopping rule.

Equation 4 (timeout fallback) provides a graceful degradation when no accumulator reaches the boundary within T_max steps, defaulting to the action with the highest accumulated evidence at the time limit.

Equation 5 (reward history update) implements exponential moving average learning, updating only the chosen action's reward trace r_bar_i with recency weight γ, allowing past reward context to bias future drift rates.
Parameter a (decision boundary) controls the evidence threshold required for action commitment; typical values [0.5, 2.0] balance speed-accuracy tradeoff in perceptual decision-making.

Taste and palatability are redundantly represented as the same underlying value (food.palatability), suggesting the current attribute schema conflates hedonic valuation with nutritional signaling; disaggregating these into distinct environment-provided properties would allow study of tradeoffs between immediate reward and long-term utility.
Parameter σ (diffusion coefficient) scales within-trial stochastic noise; standard value 0.1 follows Ratcliff (1978) convention for unit-normalized noise processes.

Run bd5e6d0d-382f-46f9-9900-2994e0d5eed3 contradicted memory 9c1ba79a-e06f-4041-bcaf-09269309ef58: Parameter σ (diffusion coefficient) is fixed at 1.0 by scaling convention, normalizing the stochastic noise in the accumulation process. This convention is established in both Ratcliff & Tuerlinckx (2002) and Bogacz et al. (2006). → Parameter σ (diffusion coefficient) scales within-trial stochastic noise; standard value 0.1 follows Ratcliff (1978) convention for unit-normalized noise processes.
Parameter z_0 (starting point) initializes accumulators at 0.5 * a = 0.75 for unbiased decisions; deviations from 0.5 * a introduce decision bias.
Parameter Δt (integration time-step) discretizes the Wiener process; value 0.01 follows Bogacz et al. (2006) for numerical stability.
Parameter T_max (maximum deliberation steps) prevents indefinite accumulation loops; value 100 is empirically chosen for grid navigation timescales.
Parameter k_res (resource proximity scaling) weights the influence of spatial proximity to resources on drift rate; derived from postulate P2 linking stimulus salience to evidence accumulation.
Parameter k_eat (eat action base drift) sets the baseline desirability (drift contribution) of the eat action when a resource is present, allowing tuning of foraging motivation.
Parameter γ (recency weighting) controls the decay rate of reward history in exponential moving average; value 0.9 emphasizes recent rewards in drift computation.
The formulation implements a multi-action parallel evidence accumulation model combining classical drift-diffusion decision-making with resource-driven utility computation and reward learning.
Equation 1 computes drift rate as a linear scaling of the perceptual signal: v_i = k_v * s_i, where s_i is inverse-distance to nearest resource or binary resource indicator.
Equation 2 computes choice probability from drift rate using the closed-form first-passage time solution for a Wiener process with boundaries at 0 and a; P_i approaches 0.5 when drift is zero.
Equation 3 computes expected decision time using the closed-form expression from Bogacz et al. (2006); for small drift rates, it reduces to the limit a^2 / (2σ^2).
Equation 4 combines three components: DDM-predicted accuracy (P_i), a time cost penalty (−λ * T_bar_i), and learned utility (w_Q * Q_i) into a composite action value.

All four facts converge on a single canonical pattern: the simulation's temporal index is exclusively sourced from perception['step'] (or perception.step), making this field the sole state-time anchor for Q-table indexing and decision logic — any future research run should treat perception['step'] as a critical, non-redundant input feature.
Equation 6 performs temporal difference Q-learning: Q_i is updated toward the observed reward with learning rate α, implementing online action-value estimation.
Boundary separation a (default 1.5) comes from Ratcliff & McKoon (2008) and controls the decision threshold; larger a increases accuracy at the cost of slower decisions.

Parameter z (relative starting point) is set to 0.5 (z = a/2) from Ratcliff (1978) and Ratcliff & Rouder (1998), representing an unbiased starting position equidistant from both boundaries with no a priori bias toward either boundary.
Diffusion coefficient σ (default 0.1) is a scaling convention from Ratcliff (1978) that determines noise in the evidence accumulation process.
Drift rate scaling constant k_v (default 1.0) maps perceptual signal strength to drift rate units.
Time cost penalty λ (default 0.1) implements speed-accuracy trade-off by penalizing slow decisions; derived from P3 drift-diffusion literature.
Weight on accuracy w_P (default 1.0) controls the relative importance of expected accuracy component in composite action value.
Weight on learned utility w_Q (default 0.5) controls the relative importance of Q-learning term in composite action value.
Perceptual signal s_i is task-dependent: for movement actions it is inverse-distance to nearest resource (1/(1+d_i)), for eat it is 1 if resource present or −0.5 if absent, and for stay it is 0.
Drift rate v_i scales linearly with perceptual signal s_i via Eq. 1, creating a direct mapping from perception to evidence accumulation rate.
Choice probability P_i increases monotonically with drift rate v_i, implementing the principle that stronger perceptual evidence leads to higher accuracy.
Expected decision time T_bar_i has non-linear dependence on drift rate v_i; larger drift reduces decision time, but the effect is bounded by the tanh saturation.

Composite value V_i trades off three objectives: maximizing accuracy (positive term w_P * P_i), minimizing decision time (negative term −λ * T_bar_i), and leveraging learned reward structure (positive term w_Q * Q_i).
Action selection probability π_i follows softmax distribution over composite values V_i, making the policy stochastic with temperature β controlling softmax sharpness.
Hunger dynamics equation (Eq. 1) updates hunger at each step as a function of hunger rise rate η, satiation amount φ, and whether the agent ate, clipped to remain within [0, 1].
Decision-time desirability equation (Eq. 2) computes outcome values by modulating food reward r_food by current hunger h, while non-food outcomes have value equal to step cost c_step.
Model-based Q-value equation (Eq. 3) computes goal-directed action values as the expected value of possible outcomes weighted by the learned transition model probabilities.
Transition model update equation (Eq. 4) uses Hebbian-style learning to refine the agent's estimate of action–outcome contingencies, with learning rate α_T.
TD prediction error equation (Eq. 5) computes reward prediction error as the difference between received reward plus discounted future value and current Q-estimate.
Model-free Q-value update equation (Eq. 6) performs temporal-difference learning on cached Q-values using the prediction error δ and learning rate α_MF.
Arbitration weight equation (Eq. 7) decays from 1 toward 0 via exponential function of visit count N(s), with decay rate controlled by habit formation rate λ.
Net action value equation (Eq. 8) implements dual-system control by mixing model-based and model-free Q-values, with weight ω shifting from goal-directed to habitual as experience accumulates.
Model-free learning rate α_MF (default 0.10) is set at standard TD-learning values from literature to control adaptation speed of cached habitual values.
Transition model learning rate α_T (default 0.20) is set higher than α_MF following Rangel (2013) and postulate P2, reflecting faster learning in model-based systems.

Temporal discount factor γ = 0.95 (from Rangel et al., 2008) follows standard RL convention for moderate future discounting in value estimation. It determines the agent's present-value weighting of future rewards, reflecting its preference for immediate versus delayed rewards.

λ = 0.05: Habit formation rate from Rangel (2013); controls the timescale of the gradual shift from goal-directed to habitual control over repeated rewarded trials, tuned to match overtraining behavioral transitions (postulate P4).
Hunger rise rate λ_H / η (default 0.02 per step) determines how quickly hunger accumulates when the agent is not eating; calibrated so the agent becomes significantly hungry over ~50 steps.

The formulation implements dual-system reinforcement learning: model-free (habitual) Q_MF learns cached values via TD(0), while model-based (goal-directed) Q_MB computes values prospectively using a learned transition model.
Arbitration between goal-directed and habitual control is deterministic and experience-dependent: ω decays with N(s) such that repeated state visits progressively shift control toward habitual Q_MF.

The agent's decision-time desirability for food outcomes is multiplicatively coupled to hunger h, implementing a reinforcer-specific satiation mechanism where motivation decreases as hunger is satisfied.
Equation 1 computes hunger dynamics by incrementing hunger by rate η per time step and reducing hunger by φ when the eating action is taken, clipped to [0,1].
Equation 2 defines decision-time desirability as the product of hunger state h and base food reward r_food for the food outcome, or the step cost c_step otherwise.
Equation 3 computes goal-directed value as the expected value over outcomes, weighted by the learned transition model p_hat and their decision-time desirabilities r_D.
Equation 4 implements habit strength accumulation via Euler discretization where habits grow proportional to τ_H when positive reward is received and decay constantly at rate τ_D.

Equation 5 models cognitive control capacity as a dynamical system that recovers toward C_max at rate ρ when goal-directed mode is not used, and is depleted by ξ when goal-directed control is engaged.

Equation 6 determines the probability p_GD of engaging goal-directed control as a linear scaling of cognitive capacity C_t, but only if C_t exceeds the threshold θ; otherwise, habitual control is forced.
Equation 7 implements stochastic system arbitration: with probability p_GD, composite urgency U equals goal-directed value V_GD; otherwise it equals habit strength H.
Equation 8 selects actions stochastically via softmax over the composite urgency U(s,a) with temperature parameter β (inverse temperature).
Equation 9 updates the learned transition model p_hat via delta-rule learning with learning rate α_T, moving the estimate toward the observed outcome indicator.
Parameter τ_H (habit accumulation rate = 0.03) reflects slow growth of stimulus–response associations during overtraining, sourced from Rangel et al., 2008 and postulate P4.
Parameter τ_D (habit decay rate = 0.005) implements slow decay of unused habits, reflecting the stability of learned associations.
Parameter α_T (transition model learning rate = 0.20) reflects rapid model-based learning, sourced from Rangel, 2013.
Parameter C_max (max cognitive control capacity = 1.0) establishes the normalized upper bound for cognitive resources.
Parameter ρ (control recovery rate = 0.05) controls per-step recovery of cognitive capacity toward C_max when habitual control is used.
Parameter ξ (control depletion per GD override = 0.10) quantifies the computational cost of engaging goal-directed deliberation, sourced from Rangel et al., 2008.
Parameter θ (override threshold = 0.15) sets the minimum cognitive control capacity required to engage the goal-directed system.
Parameter η (hunger rise rate = 0.02) controls per-step increase in hunger when the eating action is not taken.
Parameter β (inverse temperature = 5.0) controls the stochasticity of action selection via softmax; higher β makes selection more deterministic.
The formulation implements goal-directed versus habitual control as a binary-stochastic arbitration where the choice between systems depends on depletable cognitive capacity C_t.
Repeated goal-directed deliberation depletes C_t, causing the agent to fall back on habits, implementing the behavioral shift from flexible goal-directed to rigid habitual control after overtraining.

Habit strengths H(s,a) accumulate only from positive rewards and decay continuously, capturing the asymmetry that outcome devaluation immediately affects goal-directed behavior (via V_GD) but not habits (Postulates P2 and P3).
The learned transition model p_hat enables prospective model-based computation of V_GD and is updated whenever an action generates an outcome, implementing flexible model learning.
Cognitive control capacity C_t operates as a depletable resource analogous to prefrontal executive function, recovered passively when habitual control is used and consumed by deliberative control.
Equation 1 computes next-step energy reserves by subtracting basal metabolic cost α_E and adding nutritional gain c_food if food was consumed, bounded to [0,1].
Equation 2 computes hunger drive as a sigmoid function of energy deficit (E_set - E_t), scaled by sensitivity parameter k_H.
Equation 3 couples attribute weights inversely: caloric weight w_c,t equals hunger H_t, while effort weight w_e,t equals 1 - H_t.
Equation 4 computes the value of eating as the product of caloric weight, base food reward, and hunger drive.
Equation 3 specifies that leptin proxy is proportional to energy stores through power law coupling with exponent k_L.
Equation 5 computes movement value as distance-discounted food reward weighted by caloric drive, plus effort cost weighted by effort sensitivity.
Equation 6 sets staying in place at zero value, making it a default comparison baseline.

The repeated re-discovery of perception['step'] across pipeline stages suggests the current state representation may be overly dependent on step count as a proxy for state, which could limit generalization; future runs should investigate whether additional perceptual features beyond the step index are being underutilized in Q-table state construction.
Parameter α_E (basal metabolic cost = 0.01) represents per-step energy loss from Jacquier (2016) adapted to grid timescale.

The formulation integrates homeostatic principles (energy regulation via E_set) with goal-directed multi-attribute valuation to model appetite-dependent food choice.
Decision logic uses argmax action selection (greedy policy) based on context-dependent values computed from hunger drive and resource distance.
Equation 1 computes energy dynamics by decreasing energy stores via basal metabolic cost and increasing them proportionally when eating occurs.

The model's computational rules partition cleanly into two mechanistic subsystems: a dopaminergic wanting/prediction-error system (R1, R2) grounded solely in Postulate P2, and an alliesthesia-based liking/hunger system (R3, R4, R5, R7) grounded solely in Postulate P4 — suggesting these two affective valuation streams are theoretically independent and could be selectively manipulated or lesioned in future simulations.

Learning rate α (default 0.10) controls the magnitude of Q-value updates (standard RL value-update stepsize); typical range is (0, 1]. Value set to 0.10 per Rangel et al. (2008), noting gradual incremental learning, and consistent with the Sutton & Barto convention for the standard RL learning rate.
Ghrelin's positive modulation of food-related Q-values is amplified when energy stores are depleted and ghrelin rises.
Leptin's negative modulation of food-related Q-values suppresses eating when energy reserves are sufficient.
The hormonal modulator multiplicatively scales food-seeking Q-values during action selection, implementing state-dependent value modulation.
TD-error drives incremental Q-value learning, allowing the agent to adjust value estimates based on reward prediction violations.
When ghrelin is high and leptin is low, the modulator M_t >> 1, strongly amplifying food-seeking behavior.
When satiated (low ghrelin, high leptin), the modulator M_t ≈ 1 or M_t < 1, suppressing food-seeking and enabling exploration of other actions.
Energy store dynamics R1: E_t decays at basal metabolic rate α_E, increases by c_food when eating occurs, bounded to [0, 1] (i.e., E_max = 1).
Hunger drive H_t increases at rate η_H over time and decreases by κ_H when eating occurs, bounded to [0, 1].
Goal-directed value for eating is the product of hunger drive H_t and base food reward r_food, making it hunger-sensitive.
Goal-directed value for movement decreases with distance d_t(a) and is penalized by step cost c_step.
Stay action has zero goal-directed value regardless of hunger state.
Habitual reward R_t^H is r_food when eating, otherwise c_step, and is not modulated by hunger (devaluation-insensitive).
Prediction error δ_t computes temporal difference between current reward plus discounted future Q-value and current Q-value estimate.
Habitual Q-value Q^H is updated via TD learning with learning rate α_Q, implementing stimulus-response caching.
Arbitration weight ω_t decays linearly with eating experience count n_eat at rate λ_ω, transitioning from goal-directed to habitual control.
Initial goal-directed weight ω_0 is set to 0.80, reflecting dominance of model-based control early in learning.
Integrated value combines goal-directed and habitual values via ω_t, producing a mixture policy.
Base policy is computed via softmax over integrated values with inverse temperature β = 5.0.
Pavlovian override probability is p_Pav = 0.20 when food is present, causing direct consumption with 20% probability.
Final policy mixes base softmax policy with Pavlovian override, producing both exploratory and cue-driven behavior.
Basal metabolic cost α_E = 0.01 is adapted from Jacquier (2016) and represents energy loss independent of behavior.
Energy gain from eating c_food = 0.30 is normalized to maintain bounded energy state.
Hunger rise rate η_H = 0.02 per step is adapted from Jacquier (2016) and drives background motivation.
Hunger reduction on eating κ_H = 0.40 implements post-prandial satiation, reducing hunger below zero (clamped).
Habitual learning rate α_Q = 0.05 is slower than goal-directed updates, reflecting persistence of cached stimulus-response associations.

Energy set-point E_set = 0.50 is the homeostatic reference but is not used in the current formulation (reserved for future extensions).

Early-stage behavior exhibits devaluation sensitivity: satiated agents (H_t ≈ 0) assign near-zero value to eating due to goal-directed dominance.
Late-stage behavior exhibits habitual overeating: agents consume food whenever nearby because ω_t ≈ 0 and Q^H is hunger-independent.
Pavlovian override is always active when food is present, causing occasional eating even when satiated and in goal-directed mode.
Formulation 3 captures three-controller architecture from Rangel (2013) with goal-directed, habitual, and Pavlovian systems.

Hunger drive H(t) grows logistically toward 1 when no food is being consumed, with a natural ceiling created by the term (1 − H), and drops sharply upon food intake according to the satiation rate k_sat.
Associative strength V(s) is driven toward the reward signal r(t) during presence at state s, implementing the Rescorla–Wagner learning rule weighted by the learning rate α_V.
Associative strength V(s) decays at rate η during unreinforced CS presentations (extinction learning), implementing slow decay of Pavlovian associations.

Effective drive E(t) is computed as the multiplicative product of hunger H(t), drive coupling constant μ, and the maximum perceived associative strength V_perceived(t), implementing homeostatic modulation.
Approach direction is determined entirely by gradient ascent on the learned associative value landscape V(s), implementing model-free stimulus-bound behavior without learned action–outcome mappings.
The associative learning rate α_V (default 0.10) sets the speed of Pavlovian conditioning within the Rescorla–Wagner framework.
The hunger growth rate c_H (default 0.02) controls the rate at which hunger accrues metabolically per time step.

The satiation rate k_sat (default 0.25) determines the magnitude of hunger reduction upon food intake.
The drive coupling constant μ (default 1.5) amplifies the multiplicative interaction between hunger and associative strength in effective drive computation.
The approach threshold θ_approach (default 0.3) defines the minimum effective drive required to initiate Pavlovian approach behavior.
The eat threshold θ_eat (default 0.5) defines the minimum effective drive required to trigger eating when food is present.
The extinction decay rate η (default 0.01) sets the rate of Pavlovian association decay during unreinforced CS presentations.

The approach policy is deterministic and threshold-based, producing binary approach/no-approach decisions that transition sharply as hunger or CS value change.
An unconditioned eating response is triggered when H(t) > 0.8 and food is present, reflecting very high hunger overriding weak CS associations.
Explicit extinction dynamics allow associative strength to decay slowly when CS is presented without food, capturing the outcome-insensitive nature of Pavlovian conditioning.
The ODE framework naturally models continuous temporal dynamics, with hunger accumulation reflecting time since last meal and gradual conditioning over trials.

The default value of gamma_H is 0.6 (sourced from Rangel 2013: hunger amplifies taste/hedonic weight).
The default value of delta is 0.7 (sourced from Rangel 2013: abstract attributes are down-weighted under low control).
The default value of eta is 0.01 (sourced from simulation convention: slow metabolic timescale).
The default value of R_food is 0.3 (sourced from simulation convention).
The default value of lam is 0.05 (sourced from standard reinforcement learning rate for attentional updates).
The default value of epsilon is 0.05 (sourced from Rangel 2013: prevents any attribute from being fully ignored).
alpha_imm and alpha_abs are always constrained to [0.05, 0.95] and renormalized to sum to 1 after each update.

The decision logic proceeds in two phases per step: a DECIDE phase (compute weights, attribute values, action values, sample action) and an UPDATE phase (update hunger and attention after action execution).
The formulation 'Attribute-Based Evidence Accumulation (Drift-Diffusion)' belongs to the drift-diffusion-model paradigm.
Drift rates for each action are computed as the weighted linear sum of two attribute values: immediate proximity attribute (a_imm) and abstract palatability attribute (a_abs).

content: Formulation.drift-diffusion-model:classical-wiener-process-with-per-action-accumulators -[HAS_PARAMETER]-> Parameter.drift-diffusion-model:classical-wiener-process-with-per-action-accumulators:sigma
Parameter phi has default value 1.5, representing dlPFC amplification of abstract attribute weighting (Rangel 2013).
Parameter tau_w has default value 5.0, providing moderate inertia in weight tracking dynamics.
Parameter beta has default value 5.0, as a standard softmax inverse temperature for action selection.
Parameter alpha_Q has default value 0.1, as a standard TD learning rate.
Parameter gamma has default value 0.9, as a standard temporal discount factor for Q-learning.
The Attribute-Reweighting Algebraic Model belongs to the dlPFC self-control modulation paradigm based on Rangel (2013).
The formulation is an algebraic weighted-sum valuation model where vmPFC computes composite chosen value (CCV) from taste and health attribute weights.
dlPFC dynamically modulates the relative weight of the health (abstract) attribute in vmPFC via a coupling variable C.
Health is not directly perceived; it is derived as an inverse proxy: health = 1 - palatability.
The conflict signal K is computed as the taste advantage of the tastiest food over the healthiest food when they differ.
K is zero when the tastiest and healthiest resources are the same item.
dlPFC coupling C is computed as: sigmoid(lambda_K * K) * G * (1 - D), combining conflict sensitivity, goal activation, and available executive capacity.

P5 (Assumption) specifies that internal hunger modulates goal-directed desirability; eta governs the dynamics of the hunger drive that feeds into r_D_food = h * r_food."}

metadata.derivation_chain: eta (=0.02) is the per-step hunger rise rate. P5 (Assumption) specifies that internal hunger modulates goal-directed desirability; eta governs the dynamics of the hunger drive that feeds into r_D_food = h * r_food.

content: Formulation.goal-directed-vs-habitual-control:ode-based-habit-strength-with-continuous-arbitration-dynamics -[HAS_PARAMETER]-> Parameter.goal-directed-vs-habitual-control:ode-based-habit-strength-with-continuous-arbitration-dynamics:c-max
B1 (testable): Without eating for 30 steps, G should increase by approximately lambda_G per step.
B2 (testable): Setting G = 0.8 then eating should drop G by kappa_G to approximately 0.3.
B3 (testable): With G = 0.9 and E = 0.1, computing M should yield M > 2.0.
B4 (testable): With G = 0.1 and E = 0.9, computing M should yield M < 1.0.
B5 (testable): After 500 training steps, Q-values for move_toward_food actions should exceed those for move_away_food actions in states with nearby food.
B6 (testable): Fixing Q-values and varying M between 2.5 (hungry) and 0.5 (satiated) should produce significantly different P(eat) under the softmax.
The ate_flag is derived from perception['last_action_result']: 1 if eat succeeded (resource consumed), else 0.
Food-at-cell is true if any food item in the perception list shares the agent's (x, y) position.
The current step index is obtained from perception['step'].

The reward source for this formulation is the eat action triggering a ConsumeEffect with reward=1.0 in the environment. This raw reward value is used only as a signal for the ate_flag and is not directly used as a training reward.
The internal reward R is computed via rule R5 using hormonal modulator M, not the raw environment reward.

Set to 0.3 to provide meaningful satiation per food event."}

metadata.derivation_chain: P2 requires current desirability r^D(o|s) to reflect the agent's current need state. phi is the hunger reduction upon eating (h -= phi), enabling satiation and thus outcome devaluation that immediately affects goal-directed values. Set to 0.3 to provide meaningful satiation per food event.

content: The health weight is modulated as: w_h = w_h0 + gamma_C * C, clamped to [0, 1]; taste weight is its complement.
At baseline (C=0), health weight is 0.2 and taste weight is 0.8, reflecting hedonic dominance.
At maximum coupling (C=1), health weight reaches 0.8 and taste weight falls to 0.2.
Composite chosen value for an eat action is: CCV = w_tau * palatability + w_h * (1 - palatability).
For movement actions, CCV is computed using the nearest reachable food discounted by distance_discount^dist.
Model uses softmax action selection with inverse temperature beta = 5.0 over composite chosen values.

P1 stipulates that the two valuation systems feed into a common action-selection mechanism; beta modulates the exploitation-exploration tradeoff in that shared softmax mechanism (Rangel, Camerer & Montague, 2008)."}

metadata.derivation_chain: beta (=5.0) is the inverse temperature for softmax action selection. P1 stipulates that the two valuation systems feed into a common action-selection mechanism; beta modulates the exploitation-exploration tradeoff in that shared softmax mechanism (Rangel, Camerer & Montague, 2008).

P2 specifies that non-food outcomes have desirability r_D_nofood = c_step, encoding the small cost of movement/time in the goal-directed value computation."}

content: Parameter.homeostatic-regulation-of-food-valuation:drive-reduction-ode-with-goal-directed-valuation:r-food -[DERIVES_FROM]-> Postulate.homeostatic-regulation-of-food-valuation:P1 {"derivation_chain": "P1 grounds food value computation in hunger state. r_food = 1.0 is the normalized base food reward from Rangel (2013), serving as the scalar anchor in the multi-attribute value equations V_eat = w_c * r_food * H."}

metadata.derivation_chain: P1 grounds food value computation in hunger state. r_food = 1.0 is the normalized base food reward from Rangel (2013), serving as the scalar anchor in the multi-attribute value equations V_eat = w_c * r_food * H.

content: The conflict noise scaling parameter kappa defaults to 0.5, grounded in Rangel (2013) which predicts attribute conflict increases reaction time and noise.
The maximum deliberation steps T_max defaults to 10, enforcing bounded rationality by capping the evidence accumulation race.
The attribute weight learning rate beta_w defaults to 0.05, following standard reinforcement learning conventions.
The hunger rise rate eta defaults to 0.01, reflecting a slow metabolic timescale by simulation convention.
The hunger restoration from eating R_food defaults to 0.3, set by simulation convention.
Attribute weights are computed from hunger state: w_imm = (1 + H_t) / (2 + H_t) and w_abs = 1.0 / (2 + H_t), ensuring w_imm + w_abs = 1 always.
Hunger monotonically increases the immediate-attribute weight; no attentional learning is involved in weight computation — weights are purely state-driven.
Evidence accumulators are reset to 0 at the start of each simulation step's deliberation phase.

The first accumulator to reach or exceed threshold theta is selected as the winning action; if no accumulator crosses threshold within T_max steps, the action with the highest accumulated evidence is selected.
Attribute weight updates use reward prediction error: RPE = reward - mu_selected, with weights adjusted proportionally to each attribute value of the selected action.
After RPE-based weight updates, both w_imm and w_abs are clipped to a minimum of 0.05 and then renormalized to sum to 1.
Hunger state H_t rises by eta each simulation step and drops by R_food when food is consumed, clamped to [0, 1].

metadata.derivation_chain: P2 specifies that goal-directed value uses outcome desirability r^D(o|s). r_food is the base reward magnitude for food outcomes, entering V_GD via hunger-scaled desirability r_D_food = h * r_food, and is normalized to match the environment's ConsumeEffect reward.

content: Parameter.homeostatic-regulation-of-food-valuation:drive-reduction-ode-with-goal-directed-valuation:k-h -[DERIVES_FROM]-> Postulate.homeostatic-regulation-of-food-valuation:P1 {"derivation_chain": "P1 establishes the sigmoid mapping from energy deficit to hunger drive: H = sigmoid(k_H * (E_set - E)). k_H = 4.0 is the sensitivity parameter tuned so the sigmoid spans [0,1] meaningfully across the deficit range [-0.5, 0.5], making hunger responsive but not binary."}

metadata.derivation_chain: P1 establishes the sigmoid mapping from energy deficit to hunger drive: H = sigmoid(k_H * (E_set - E)). k_H = 4.0 is the sensitivity parameter tuned so the sigmoid spans [0,1] meaningfully across the deficit range [-0.5, 0.5], making hunger responsive but not binary.

content: Formulation.pavlovian-control-of-food-approach:incentive-salience-dual-process-agent-wantingliking-dissociation -[HAS_PARAMETER]-> Parameter.pavlovian-control-of-food-approach:incentive-salience-dual-process-agent-wantingliking-dissociation:alpha-w

Formulation.pavlovian-control-of-food-approach:incentive-salience-dual-process-agent-wantingliking-dissociation -[HAS_PARAMETER]-> Parameter.pavlovian-control-of-food-approach:incentive-salience-dual-process-agent-wantingliking-dissociation:w-w
The blended action value is U_a = 0.5 * V_o + 0.5 * Q_a, reflecting equal weighting of goal-directed and habitual systems (rule R7, postulate P4).
Action selection uses softmax with inverse temperature beta over blended values U_a (rule R8, postulate P2).
Q-value updates follow standard Q-learning via TD(0): Q(s,a) ← Q(s,a) + alpha_Q * [reward + γ * max_a Q(s', a) - Q(s,a)]. This update rule is defined by rule R9 and postulate P4.
State for Q-table lookup is discretized as (perception['x'], perception['y']).
The ate indicator is set to 1 if selected_action == 'eat' and reward > 0, else 0.
The step counter is read from perception['step'] and used for Q-table state tracking.
B1 (testable): Sustained decision-making without eating should cause K_t < K_0, w_imm > w_abs, and w_imm higher than initial — verifiable by running 200 steps without eating.
B2 (testable): After depleting K to 0.1 and running 100 further steps with easy food, K_t should recover toward K_0 and w_abs should increase.
B3 (testable): A sudden spike in H_t should not cause w_imm to jump to its new equilibrium immediately; the rate of change of w_imm is bounded by tau_w.
B4 (testable): After 500 steps in a stable environment, Q-values near food locations should be higher and navigation efficiency toward food should improve.

Higher alpha_W (0.12) vs alpha_L reflects the empirically faster plasticity of dopamine circuits."}

metadata.derivation_chain: P2 posits dopaminergic wanting learning via RPE (R1, R2). alpha_W is the learning rate for this fast phasic dopamine-mediated update: W(s) += alpha_W * delta_W. Higher alpha_W (0.12) vs alpha_L reflects the empirically faster plasticity of dopamine circuits.

Wanting dominates (>0.5) per incentive salience theory; w_W + w_L = 1.0."}

metadata.derivation_chain: P3 posits weighted combination of wanting and liking. w_W (0.6) is the relative weight of wanting in Q_total = w_W * Q_W + w_L * Q_L (R8). Wanting dominates (>0.5) per incentive salience theory; w_W + w_L = 1.0.

content: Formulation.pavlovian-control-of-food-approach:incentive-salience-dual-process-agent-wantingliking-dissociation -[HAS_PARAMETER]-> Parameter.pavlovian-control-of-food-approach:incentive-salience-dual-process-agent-wantingliking-dissociation:alpha-l
B5 (testable): With H_t=0.95 and K_t=0.05, the agent should prefer the nearest food over high-palatability distant food in more than 70% of trials.
B6 (testable): When the agent is on the same cell as food with H_t=0.5 and K_t=0.5, the eat action should be selected with high probability.
The ODE dynamics for w_imm and w_abs introduce temporal inertia, preventing instant behavioral strategy switches.
Cognitive control depletion over long episodes causes a gradual drift from quality-seeking to proximity-seeking behavior.
The formulation is grounded in Rangel (2013) on dlPFC top-down modulation (Nature Neuroscience, 16(12), 1717-1724).
The dual-system blending in R7 is grounded in Rangel, Camerer & Montague (2008) on goal-directed vs. habitual systems (Nature Reviews Neuroscience, 9(7), 545-556).
Parameter eta_H has default value 0.01, sourced from simulation metabolic timescale.
Parameter R_food has default value 0.3, sourced from simulation convention.
Parameter tau_K has default value 50.0, sourced from Rangel (2013) on slow dlPFC recovery.
Parameter K_0 has default value 0.5, representing moderate baseline self-regulation.
Parameter c_K has default value 0.02, modelling ego-depletion per step (Rangel 2013).

content: Expected behavior B3 is testable via a controlled symmetric environment experiment.
Expected behavior B4 (reward history biases future decisions) can be tested by giving the agent 10 eat rewards and asserting r_bar['eat'] > r_bar['move_up'].
Expected behavior B4 is testable because the exponential moving average update in R5 accumulates reward signal in r_bar.
Expected behavior B5 (decision time varies with evidence quality) can be tested by comparing mean T_d when food is directly adjacent versus far away.
Expected behavior B5 is testable because higher drift rates from high proximity cause faster boundary crossings (lower T_d).
